findings.title: Negative/zero qty bypasses stock check
findings.description: In addOrderItems, the check `product.countInStock < item.qty` passes for negative/zero qty values because stock (≥0) is never < -5 or < 0. If qty=-5, `$inc: { countInStock: -(-5) }` INCREASES stock by 5 instead of decreasing it. The server-side price recalculation uses `itemTotal * item.qty` producing a negative subtotal, resulting in totalPrice=0 via Math.max(0, ...). Attacker gets free items and inflates stock simultaneously.
findings.fix: Add a guard at the top of addOrderItems: `if (orderItems.some(item => item.qty <= 0 || !Number.isInteger(item.qty))) { res.status(400); throw new Error('Invalid item quantity'); }`. Also validate qty > 0 in the Product schema.

findings.title: updateOrderStatus cancels without refund/stock-restore
findings.description: VALID_TRANSITIONS allows 'Cancelled' from 'Placed' and 'Preparing'. When an admin or restaurant_owner sets status to 'Cancelled' via PUT /:id/status, the code (line 624) only does `order.orderStatus = status` and saves. It does NOT restore product stock ($inc countInStock), does NOT process wallet refunds, does NOT free the delivery partner, and does NOT delete CouponUsage records. The cancelOrder function (line 856) has all this logic but updateOrderStatus bypasses it entirely. This causes financial loss and inventory corruption.
findings.fix: Remove 'Cancelled' from VALID_TRANSITIONS so cancellation can ONLY go through the /:id/cancel route which handles refunds and stock restore. Alternatively, extract the stock-restore + refund logic into a shared helper and call it from updateOrderStatus when status === 'Cancelled'.

findings.title: createRazorpayOrder/verifyPayment lacks ownership check
findings.description: Both createRazorpayOrder and verifyPayment accept an orderId from the request body but never verify that the authenticated user OWNS that order. Any authenticated user can pay for any order they know the ID of. While the amount is validated from the DB, the authorization gap means User A can pay for User B's order and have it marked as paid. The verifyPayment endpoint (line 78) also lacks ownership check — it only verifies Razorpay signature and notes match.
findings.fix: In createRazorpayOrder, after finding the order (line 47), add: `if (dbOrder.user.toString() !== req.user._id.toString() && req.user.role !== 'admin') { return res.status(403).json({...}); }`. In verifyPayment, before marking paid (line 150), add the same check: `if (order.user.toString() !== req.user._id.toString() && req.user.role !== 'admin') return res.status(403).json({...});`.

findings.title: Wallet-only refund for Online payments
findings.description: cancelOrder refunds Online payments to the user's wallet (line 908: $inc walletBalance) instead of initiating a Razorpay refund via the payment gateway. The comment says 'Refund to Wallet instead of Payment Gateway' but this means the user who paid ₹500 via credit card gets ₹500 in SwadKart wallet credits instead of money back to their card. SwadKart keeps the actual payment and forces the user to spend credits within the platform. This is financially unfair to users and potentially violates payment card network rules.
findings.fix: Add Razorpay refund initiation before the wallet credit: use `instance.payments.refund(paymentId, { amount: Math.round(order.totalPrice * 100) })`. Only fall back to wallet credit if the Razorpay refund fails. Store the refund ID in order.refundStatus.

findings.title: getMyDeliveryOrders missing pagination
findings.description: The query at line 24 loads ALL orders for a delivery partner with no limit, skip, or pagination. A partner with hundreds or thousands of past deliveries will receive the entire dataset in one response. This causes high memory usage on the server, slow response times, and excessive bandwidth consumption.
findings.fix: Add pagination: parse page/limit from req.query, apply .skip() and .limit() similar to getMyOrders (line 732-740 of orderController.js). Also add .lean() which is already present.

findings.title: Admin role on /deliver route always gets rejected
findings.description: The route PUT /:id/deliver (orderRoutes.js:103-107) permits both 'admin' and 'delivery_partner' roles. However, the controller at line 204-208 checks `order.deliveryPartner.toString() !== req.user._id.toString()` which always fails for admins since the deliveryPartner is never the admin's ID. Admin can never complete a delivery via this route despite being authorized to access it.
findings.fix: Add an admin bypass: `if (req.user.role === 'admin') { /* skip OTP and partner check */ }` or remove 'admin' from authorizeRoles in the route if admins should not use this endpoint directly.

findings.title: getMyRestaurantOrders missing pagination and limit
findings.description: The query at line 766-772 fetches ALL orders for a restaurant without any limit/skip/pagination. For popular restaurants with thousands of orders, this response can grow very large and slow. Compare to getOrders (admin) which has proper pagination at line 793-805.
findings.fix: Add page/limit parsing and apply .skip()/.limit() to the query. Return metadata alongside data.

findings.title: createRazorpayOrder accepts frontend amount when orderId is null
findings.description: If orderId is null or omitted from the request body (line 42), the controller falls back to `verifiedAmount = Number(amount)` — the amount sent by the client. While verifyPayment eventually validates the amount against the DB order, this still creates a valid Razorpay order with the attacker-chosen amount. Combined with the missing ownership check (Finding #3), this enables creating Razorpay orders for arbitrary amounts for any order ID.
findings.fix: Make orderId required in createRazorpayOrder. Return 400 if orderId is missing or invalid. Remove the fallback to client-provided amount entirely.

findings.title: cancelOrder does not initiate actual payment gateway refund
findings.description: When an Online payment is cancelled, order.totalPrice is refunded to wallet (line 908). But the actual Razorpay payment is never refunded. This means: if the user later withdraws the wallet credit or uses it, SwadKart has paid the user twice — once via Razorpay capture and once via wallet credit. The Razorpay paymentId is known (stored in order.paymentResult.id at line 152) but no refund API call is made.
findings.fix: Before the wallet credit (line 908), add Razorpay refund: `await razorpayInstance.payments.refund(order.paymentResult.id, { amount: Math.round(order.totalPrice * 100) })`. Set refundStatus to reflect the gateway refund result, then only credit wallet if gateway refund cannot be processed.

findings.title: toggleProductStock lacks role-based authorization
findings.description: The PATCH /:id/toggle-stock route only uses `protect` middleware with no `authorizeRoles` call. While the controller does check ownership/admin internally (line 281-285), defense-in-depth is missing. Any authenticated user (including regular users) could attempt to hit this endpoint. The controller blocks unauthorized access, but this is reliance on a single layer of protection.
findings.fix: Add `authorizeRoles('admin', 'restaurant_owner')` to the route: `router.route('/:id/toggle-stock').patch(protect, authorizeRoles('admin', 'restaurant_owner'), toggleProductStock)`.

findings.title: Webhook silently ignores payment.failed events
findings.description: The razorpayWebhook handler only processes 'payment.captured' events (line 285). Razorpay also sends 'payment.failed' events but they are silently dropped. This means failed payment attempts (e.g., expired card, insufficient funds) are not logged and the user receives no notification. The order remains in 'unpaid' state without any context.
findings.fix: Add an else-if for 'payment.failed' events: log the failure reason, notify the user via socket/email, and optionally mark the order for retry or cancellation.

findings.title: resetPassword does not invalidate old sessions
findings.description: After resetPassword succeeds (line 266-269), a new token is generated (line 272) but the password hash change via pre-save hook invalidates the old token on next auth attempt. However, the old JWT may still be valid until its natural expiration (30 days). If an attacker had access to a valid JWT before the password reset, they can continue using it for up to 30 days.
findings.fix: Implement token invalidation by incrementing a `tokenVersion` counter on the user during password reset, and check this version during JWT verification in the protect middleware.
